planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, alkaline to neutral, rocky or gravelly soil in full sun; once established, evergreen sumac requires virtually no supplemental water and will decline in heavy, poorly drained soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky or gravelly, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; evergreen sumac is one of the finest drought-tolerant evergreen shrubs for dry Southwest and Texas Hill Country landscapes, holding its glossy, leathery dark-green leaves through winter and providing year-round structure. Small creamy-white flowers bloom in late summer and attract bees and other pollinators, followed by dense clusters of deep-red, slightly sticky fruits that are documented as a food source for over 100 bird species including mockingbirds, cedar waxwings, and Montezuma quail. It thrives on neglect once established and is an excellent choice for wildlife gardens, slopes, or as a large screening hedge. Avoid overwatering and heavy soils, which are the primary causes of decline.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, alkaline, limestone-derived or rocky soil in full sun; Texas pistache is native to canyon limestone and requires excellent drainage — it will not tolerate wet feet or heavy clay soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, alkaline, limestone-based or rocky soil in full sun; Texas pistache is a handsome small multi-trunked native tree of the limestone canyons of southwest Texas and northeast Mexico, prized for one of the most spectacular fall color displays of any native Texan woody plant — the compound leaves turning brilliant shades of orange, red, and scarlet before dropping. Small red fruits are eaten by birds and wildlife, and the tree is often suggested as a superior native alternative to the widely planted Chinese pistache (Pistacia chinensis). It is extremely drought tolerant once established and well suited to xeric landscapes, parking lot edges, and small residential lots in zones 7–10. Growth is slow to moderate, so purchase the largest container specimen available for faster landscape impact.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Armenian tea olive is tolerant of a range of soils but performs best with protection from harsh afternoon sun in warmer zones.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade with protection from harsh afternoon sun; Armenian tea olive is a large, handsome evergreen shrub from the Caucasus and northeastern Turkey with exceptionally large, glossy, lance-shaped leaves that give it a bolder, more tropical texture than most other osmanthus species. In spring it produces clusters of small white flowers with an intensely sweet fragrance that carries far on the breeze, followed by dark blue-black, olive-like fruits. Compared to the popular Osmanthus fragrans (sweet olive), it is considerably more cold-hardy and generally less invasive in North American landscapes. It makes an outstanding large screening hedge or specimen shrub in zones 6–9, and the bold foliage provides strong year-round structure in mixed borders.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full to partial sun; in zone 7 site against a south- or west-facing wall for winter frost protection, as hard freezes can damage the foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full to partial sun; Mexican orange blossom is one of the most popular fragrant evergreen shrubs for warm-climate gardens, producing clusters of pure white, star-shaped flowers with a powerful, sweet orange-blossom fragrance in spring and frequently reblooming again in fall. The glossy, trifoliate leaves are aromatic when crushed, releasing a sharp citrus-like scent. It is borderline hardy in zone 7 and may suffer leaf damage in hard freezes, but typically recovers vigorously from the root system — planting against a warm south-facing wall significantly improves winter survival. The compact cultivar 'Sundance' (golden-yellow foliage) and the more cold-tolerant 'Aztec Pearl' (narrower leaflets, very fragrant pink-tinged flowers) are excellent named selections.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; pearl bush blooms on old wood — prune only immediately after flowering ends, never in fall or early spring, or blooms will be lost.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to partial sun; Girald's pearl bush is a large, arching Chinese ornamental shrub native to Shaanxi province that puts on one of the most spectacular spring flower displays of any cold-hardy deciduous shrub, smothering its long arching branches with racemes of pure white, 5-petaled flowers in mid-spring just as the leaves are emerging. The flower buds are distinctly round and pearl-like before opening — the origin of the common name. It is slightly larger and somewhat hardier than the related Exochorda racemosa and can ultimately reach 12–15 feet, so allow ample space in the landscape. Prune selectively for shape only immediately after bloom, as flower buds are set on the previous season's wood; deadwood can be removed at any time.
